A2.title: How memory is stored in the brain
A2.body: Have you ever wondered how your brain keeps memories? When you learn something new, tiny cells in your brain called neurons connect to each other. They send small signals back and forth. The more you think about something, the stronger these connections become. That is why repeating a song helps you remember it. Sleep also matters a lot. When you sleep, your brain quietly reviews the day. Some memories grow stronger, and others fade away. So your brain is not really like a camera. It builds memory slowly, piece by piece, every single day.

key_phrases.phrase: is not really like a camera

B1.title: How memory is stored in the brain
B1.body: Have you ever wondered where your memories actually live? Scientists used to imagine that the brain stored memories like files in a folder, but the reality is much more interesting. Memory is built into the connections between brain cells, called neurons. Every time you learn something, certain neurons fire together, and the link between them gets a little stronger. Over time, those repeated patterns become the memory itself. This is why practice works. The more often you walk through an idea, the more reliably your brain can rebuild it later. Sleep plays a surprisingly active role too. During the night, the brain replays the day's experiences and moves the important ones into long-term storage. Things that feel emotional or useful tend to stick, while small details often quietly disappear. So memory is not a recording. It is more like a path through a forest. The more you walk it, the clearer it becomes, and the easier it is to find next time.

key_phrases.phrase: more like a path through a forest
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 오히려 숲을 지나는 길에 더 가깝다

B2.title: How memory is stored in the brain
B2.body: Imagine looking for a memory in your brain the way you look for a file on a computer. It sounds simple, but neuroscientists have learned that memory does not work that way at all. Instead of being stored in one neat place, a memory is spread across networks of brain cells. When something new happens, groups of neurons fire together, and the connections between them, called synapses, become slightly stronger. This process is known as long-term potentiation, and it is one of the foundations of learning. Each time you recall a memory, you are essentially rebuilding it by reactivating that same pattern of cells. The hippocampus, a small region deep inside the brain, plays a special role. It acts like a kind of indexing system, helping you bind together the sights, sounds, and emotions of an experience into a single coherent memory. Over time, important memories are gradually consolidated and moved into more permanent storage, while less useful ones quietly fade. Sleep is where much of the real magic happens. During slow-wave sleep, the brain replays recent activity at high speed, transferring important information from the hippocampus to the cortex, where long-term memories live. This is why pulling an all-nighter before an exam often backfires. Your brain simply has not had the time to file the day away properly. What is even more fascinating is that memory is not perfectly accurate. Every time you remember something, you slightly rewrite it, shaped by your current mood and beliefs. In a sense, your past is not fixed. It is something your brain quietly reconstructs every single time you look back at it.

key_phrases.phrase: the way you look for a file on a computer
acts like a kind of indexing system

C1.title: How memory is stored in the brain
C1.body: Have you ever paused to consider that the version of your past you carry in your head is not a recording at all, but a reconstruction? For most of the twentieth century, popular thinking treated memory as a kind of mental filing cabinet, with each event stored in its own neatly labelled drawer, waiting to be retrieved on demand. Modern neuroscience tells a very different story, and a far more interesting one. A memory does not sit in one location. It exists as a pattern of activity distributed across vast networks of neurons, encoded in the strength of the connections between them. When you learn something, the relevant neurons fire together, and the synapses linking them grow stronger through a process called long-term potentiation. Repeated exposure to the same information sharpens this pattern, while neglect lets it slowly fade. The hippocampus deserves special attention, because it acts almost like a librarian. It binds the sensory fragments of an experience, what you saw, heard, smelled and felt, into a single, retrievable episode. Over days and weeks, those episodes are gradually consolidated and transferred to the cortex, where they become part of your long-term knowledge. This transfer happens most efficiently while you sleep, particularly during deep slow-wave sleep, when the brain replays recent experiences at high speed and rewires itself accordingly. It is one reason why chronic sleep deprivation does not just leave you tired, it quietly erodes your ability to learn. Perhaps the most counterintuitive finding is that every act of remembering subtly changes the memory itself. Recall is not playback, it is reconstruction, influenced by your current emotions, beliefs, surroundings, and even the questions someone is asking you at that moment. This is why two siblings can describe the same childhood event in completely different ways, and why eyewitness testimony, despite feeling certain to the witness, can drift over time and quietly absorb later suggestions. In the end, memory is less like a static hard drive and far more like a living, breathing document, constantly being edited, gently refined, and occasionally rewritten by the slightly different person you happen to be today.

key_phrases.phrase: Have you ever paused to consider
For most of the twentieth century
Perhaps the most counterintuitive finding is

A2.title: Why impressionism emerged
A2.body: Have you ever seen a painting full of bright dots and soft light? That style is called Impressionism. It started in France in the 1860s and 1870s. Before that time, most paintings were dark and very serious. They showed kings, history, and religion. A small group of painters wanted something new. They went outside to paint the sun, the sky, and people in cafés. They painted fast and used loud colors. At first many people laughed at their work. Today, those same paintings are some of the most loved pictures in the world.

B1.title: Why impressionism emerged
B1.body: Why did so many people suddenly want to paint sunlight in the late 1800s? Impressionism did not appear out of nowhere. France was changing rapidly. Trains, gas lights, and big new boulevards transformed Paris into a modern city, and the old style of painting felt out of date. The official art world wanted polished historical scenes, but a young group of artists, including Monet, Renoir, and Degas, wanted to capture real life. They were also helped by new technology. Paint became available in small metal tubes, which meant artists could carry it outside. So they did exactly that. They went to gardens, train stations, and riversides, and tried to catch the fleeting effect of light on water and skin. The official salons rejected them at first, so they organized their own shows. Their loose brushstrokes shocked viewers used to smooth surfaces, but the movement spread quickly. Impressionism was not just a new style, it was a new attitude. It said that an ordinary moment, painted honestly, was worth more than a perfect historical scene.

key_phrases.phrase: did not appear out of nowhere

B2.title: Why impressionism emerged
B2.body: Imagine walking into a Paris gallery in 1874 and seeing what looked like a half-finished painting of a misty harbor. That painting was Claude Monet's Impression, Sunrise, and a sarcastic critic used the title to mock the whole group as mere impressionists. The name stuck, and so did one of the most influential art movements in history. To understand why Impressionism appeared exactly when it did, you have to picture mid-nineteenth century France. Paris was being rebuilt by Baron Haussmann into a city of wide boulevards and bright cafés. Trains made the countryside accessible in an afternoon, and photography was challenging painters to ask what their craft was even for, if a camera could capture a face perfectly. At the same time, the official Salon held an iron grip on which artists could succeed, favoring grand historical and mythological scenes painted in smooth, controlled brushstrokes. A small circle of painters, including Monet, Renoir, Pissarro, and Degas, refused to play that game. They were fascinated by ordinary people, by weekend boating parties, ballet rehearsals, and steam rising off train platforms. Crucially, the invention of paint sold in collapsible metal tubes let them work outdoors and chase the changing light. Their canvases were full of quick, broken strokes that the public initially found unfinished and even disrespectful. By organizing their own independent exhibitions, they bypassed the Salon entirely. Impressionism, then, was not only about color and light. It was a quiet rebellion against the idea that art must look polished and tell a noble story. It argued that the shimmer of a single afternoon, honestly observed, was worth its own place on the wall.

key_phrases.phrase: It was a quiet rebellion against

C1.title: Why impressionism emerged
C1.body: Picture a small, disgruntled crowd gathered in front of a hazy seascape in a Paris exhibition in 1874. The painting was Claude Monet's Impression, Sunrise, and a critic, hunting for a clever insult, mocked the group as nothing more than impressionists. The label, intended to wound, became the banner of one of the most consequential movements in the history of European art. To understand why Impressionism crystallized precisely when it did, one has to look beyond aesthetics and consider the broader transformation of nineteenth-century France. Paris was in the middle of Baron Haussmann's radical redesign, its medieval lanes replaced by sweeping boulevards, gas-lit cafés, and glittering shop windows. Railways had collapsed the distance between city and countryside, while the emergence of photography quietly destabilized painting's traditional claim to representational truth. If a camera could fix a likeness in seconds, what was a painter actually for? A circle of young artists, among them Monet, Renoir, Pissarro, Sisley, Morisot, and Degas, answered that question by turning their gaze toward the texture of contemporary life. They abandoned mythological tableaux in favor of dancers, laundresses, weekend rowers, and the trembling reflections on the Seine. The Académie's Salon, with its taste for polished historical pieces, repeatedly rejected their work, so they staged their own independent exhibitions, an act of institutional defiance whose significance is easy to overlook today. Technological change quietly accelerated their experiments. Industrially produced pigments in collapsible tubes liberated painters from the studio and let them chase shifting light in real time, which is precisely what Impressionism set out to capture. Their broken, vibrating brushwork, dismissed by early critics as unfinished, was in fact a careful attempt to render the way the eye truly experiences a sunlit moment, full of flicker and imprecision. In retrospect, Impressionism was less a single style than a reorientation of priorities. It insisted that fleeting sensation was a legitimate subject, that ordinary modern life deserved monumental treatment, and that artists, not institutions, should decide what counted as art. Almost every subsequent movement, from Post-Impressionism through Cubism and on into the bold experiments of early twentieth-century abstraction, would build directly on that quiet but transformative claim.

key_phrases.phrase: one has to look beyond aesthetics

A2.title: What is a B-tree
A2.body: Have you ever opened a huge book and used the table of contents to jump to a chapter? A B-tree is something like that, but for data on a computer. Imagine you have one million names and you need to find one quickly. Looking at them one by one would be far too slow. A B-tree organizes the names into a small number of big pages. Each page tells you which page to check next. After only a few jumps, you arrive at the right place. Databases love B-trees because hard drives are slow, and big jumps save a lot of time.

B1.title: What is a B-tree
B1.body: Why do almost all databases use something called a B-tree under the hood? The answer comes down to how disks actually work. Reading from a disk is hundreds of times slower than reading from memory, but each read brings back a large chunk of data at once. A B-tree is designed to make the most of that fact. It is a balanced tree where every node holds many keys, not just one or two. Because each node is wide and full, the tree stays very shallow even when it stores millions of rows. Searching becomes a small number of disk reads instead of dozens. Inserts and deletes still work in roughly logarithmic time, since the tree rebalances itself by splitting full nodes or merging empty ones. The result is a data structure that behaves well at every size, supports range queries naturally, and keeps performance predictable. That is exactly what a database needs. So when you create an index in PostgreSQL or MySQL, you are almost certainly building a B-tree behind the scenes.

key_phrases.phrase: splitting full nodes or merging empty ones
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 꽉 찬 노드를 쪼개거나 빈 노드를 합치며

B2.title: What is a B-tree
B2.body: If you have ever wondered why every major relational database, from PostgreSQL to SQLite, defaults to the same index type, the answer is the B-tree. To appreciate the design, it helps to remember a basic fact about hardware. Pulling a single byte off a spinning disk is dramatically slower than pulling that byte from memory, but the disk happily returns several kilobytes at a time once the read head is in position. A good on-disk data structure should therefore prefer wide nodes and few levels, rather than the deep, thin shapes of a classic binary search tree. The B-tree was invented in the early 1970s with exactly that hardware asymmetry in mind. Each node stores many sorted keys and many child pointers, often hundreds, sized to match a disk page. To find a value, you start at the root, do a quick binary search inside that node, and follow a single pointer to the next level. Because each step branches so widely, even a tree with a hundred million rows is typically only three or four levels deep, which translates into three or four disk reads at most. Insertions and deletions preserve balance through carefully designed split and merge operations, so the tree never gets lopsided. A small but important variant, the B-plus tree, links all the leaves together and keeps the actual data only at the bottom, which makes range scans extremely efficient. So when your query plan says index scan, you can picture a structure quietly designed around the physical reality of storage. It is a beautiful example of an algorithm shaped by its hardware.

key_phrases.phrase: defaults to the same index type
with exactly that hardware asymmetry in mind
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 바로 그 하드웨어 비대칭을 염두에 두고

C1.title: What is a B-tree
C1.body: Few data structures sit at the heart of so much practical software as the humble B-tree. Open the source of PostgreSQL, MySQL's InnoDB, SQLite, or virtually any traditional relational database, and you will find a B-tree, or its close relative the B-plus tree, quietly supporting nearly every index. To understand why, it helps to set algorithmic intuition aside for a moment and think about hardware. A spinning disk, and to a lesser extent a solid state drive, has high latency for any read but returns a large block of data per access. Memory, meanwhile, is fast but limited. Any on-disk data structure that ignores this asymmetry will struggle, no matter how elegant it looks on paper. Rudolf Bayer and Edward McCreight introduced the B-tree in 1972 with precisely this asymmetry in mind. A B-tree is a perfectly balanced search tree in which each node stores many sorted keys and many child pointers, often hundreds. The node size is deliberately matched to the underlying storage block, so a single disk read fetches a full node's worth of routing information. As a consequence, the tree's height grows logarithmically not in some abstract base two sense, but in base several hundred, meaning that even datasets of hundreds of millions of rows fit comfortably in three or four levels. Lookups, insertions, and deletions all remain logarithmic, and the tree maintains balance through carefully orchestrated split and merge operations whenever a node becomes too full or too empty. The B-plus tree, the variant actually used in most databases, pushes all real data into the leaves and links those leaves into a sorted list, which makes range scans trivially fast and turns ordered iteration into a simple pointer walk. None of this prevents alternative structures from competing in specialized roles. Log-structured merge trees, for instance, win when write throughput dominates, which is why systems like Cassandra and RocksDB prefer them. But for general-purpose, read-heavy, range-friendly workloads, the B-tree's combination of predictability, low height, and disk-aware design remains genuinely hard to beat. It is one of the cleanest examples we have of an algorithm that earns its place not by theoretical complexity alone, but by being deeply respectful of the machine it actually runs on.

key_phrases.phrase: set algorithmic intuition aside for a moment
no matter how elegant it looks on paper

A2.title: How rate limiting works
A2.body: Imagine that a small shop suddenly has a thousand customers banging on the door at the same time. The shop cannot serve them all and will probably break. Online systems face the same problem every day. Rate limiting is the rule that says, this user can send only this many requests per minute. If they go over the limit, the server politely returns an error and asks them to slow down. It protects the system from sudden traffic, bots, and bugs in client code. It is a simple idea, but it keeps many large services alive every single day.

key_phrases.phrase: It is a simple idea, but

B1.title: How rate limiting works
B1.body: Why do almost all production APIs have some form of rate limit? Without one, a single misbehaving client, a buggy script, or an aggressive scraper can flood your servers and ruin the experience for everyone else. Rate limiting solves this by setting a cap on how often a given key, usually a user ID, an API key, or an IP address, is allowed to call you in a fixed window of time. The two most common approaches are the token bucket and the fixed window. Token bucket gives each user a bucket of tokens that refills at a steady rate. Each request takes one token, and when the bucket is empty, the user is throttled. Fixed window simply counts requests inside, say, each one-minute slot. Both are usually implemented in Redis because it is fast and shared across servers. When a user exceeds their limit, the API returns HTTP 429, often with a Retry-After header that says how long to wait. The goal is never to punish users, but to keep the system stable under unexpected load.

key_phrases.phrase: have some form of rate limit
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 어떤 형태든 rate limit을 두고 있다

key_phrases.phrase: The goal is never to punish

B2.title: How rate limiting works
B2.body: If you have ever seen an HTTP 429 Too Many Requests response, you have met rate limiting in action. Behind that polite error is a small but important defense layer that keeps APIs from collapsing under their own popularity. The core idea is straightforward. You pick a key, typically a user ID, an API key, or a client IP, and you decide how many requests that key may issue in a given window. The interesting question is how to enforce that cleanly across many servers. The simplest approach, the fixed window counter, just keeps a count per key per minute. It is cheap, but it allows ugly bursts at the boundary, since a client can fire all of its quota at the end of one window and again at the start of the next. The sliding window log fixes that by recording exact request timestamps, but it is memory-hungry. Token bucket, the algorithm most large systems actually use, gives each key a bucket with a fixed capacity that refills at a steady rate. Every request removes a token, and when the bucket runs dry, the request is rejected, queued, or simply shed. Token bucket allows short bursts up to capacity while smoothing the long-term rate, which feels natural to real users. In practice, these counters live in Redis or a similar fast shared store, because the limiter must work consistently across many application servers. When a request is throttled, returning a clear 429 with a Retry-After header lets clients back off cleanly instead of hammering harder. Done well, rate limiting is invisible to good users and merciful to bad ones. It is one of the simplest and most consequential pieces of any public API.

key_phrases.phrase: have met rate limiting in action
key_phrases.meaning_ko: rate limiting이 실제로 동작하는 것을 본 셈이다

key_phrases.phrase: invisible to good users and merciful to bad ones
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 선량한 사용자에게는 보이지 않고 악성에게는 관대한

C1.title: How rate limiting works
C1.body: Few patterns in backend engineering deliver as much stability per line of code as rate limiting. A well-designed limiter quietly protects an API from buggy clients, runaway scripts, malicious traffic, and the inevitable thundering herd that follows every viral moment, while remaining nearly invisible to the well-behaved majority. The idea is deceptively simple. You choose a key, often a user ID, an API key, or a client IP, and you enforce a maximum request rate per key over some window of time. The engineering challenge lies in choosing an algorithm whose behavior under load actually matches user expectations, and in implementing it efficiently across a fleet of servers. The fixed window counter is the most naive approach. It buckets time into one-minute or one-second slots and increments a counter per key per slot. It is trivial to build, but its weakness is the boundary effect. A client can fire its entire quota in the final second of one window and again in the first second of the next, effectively doubling its rate at exactly the wrong moment. The sliding window log eliminates this artifact by storing the timestamp of each request, but it pays for that precision with memory. Token bucket, the algorithm most production systems converge on, strikes a more graceful balance. Each key owns a virtual bucket with a fixed capacity, refilled at a constant rate. A request consumes one token, and when the bucket is empty, the request is rejected, delayed, or shed. Crucially, token bucket allows brief bursts up to the bucket's capacity while strictly bounding the long-term rate, which mirrors how humans and reasonable clients actually behave. The state, whether a counter or a token count, is almost always kept in a fast in-memory store such as Redis, accessed through atomic scripts to avoid race conditions across multiple application servers. When a request is throttled, the limiter should respond with a clear HTTP 429 Too Many Requests, along with headers like Retry-After and ideally X-RateLimit-Remaining and X-RateLimit-Reset, so well-behaved clients can back off gracefully rather than retry blindly. The deepest lesson here is that rate limiting is less a defense against attackers than a contract with your callers. It communicates, in machine-readable form, how generous the system can afford to be, and gently teaches every client to live within that envelope.

key_phrases.phrase: deliver as much stability per line of code
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 코드 한 줄당 그만큼의 안정성을 제공하다

topics.title_ko: [IELTS P2] 당신에게 영향을 준 책에 대해 말해보세요

A2.title: Describe a book that has influenced you
A2.body: OK, I want to talk about a book that really stayed with me. It is called Atomic Habits, by James Clear. I read it about two years ago. A friend gave it to me when I was feeling a bit lost about my goals. The book is not about big plans. It is mainly about small habits. The writer says that if you get one percent better every day, after a year you become much better. He gives many simple examples. For example, if you want to read more, just read one page a day. That sounds easy, and it actually works for me. After reading it, I started writing for ten minutes every morning. It is a small thing, but my writing has improved a lot. So I would say this book really changed how I think about progress. Big changes feel scary, but small ones feel possible.

key_phrases.phrase: OK, I want to talk about

B1.title: Describe a book that has influenced you
B1.body: Actually, the book I want to talk about is Atomic Habits by James Clear, and it is a book that genuinely changed how I think about everyday life. I first read it about two years ago, when I was feeling stuck. I had big ambitions but very little discipline, and a close friend recommended it to me, almost like a small intervention. The book is not really about productivity, although it is shelved that way. It is about how identity is built out of tiny, repeated actions. Clear argues that you do not rise to the level of your goals, you fall to the level of your systems, and that one percent improvements compound powerfully over time. The examples are very concrete. He talks about leaving a guitar in the middle of the room so you actually practice, or wearing your running shoes the night before to make a morning run easier. What influenced me most was the idea of identity-based habits. Instead of saying I want to write a book, I tried saying I am the kind of person who writes a little every day. To be honest, that shift sounds small, but it has stuck. I now write for fifteen minutes every morning. So yes, this is the book that really shaped how I think about long-term change.

key_phrases.phrase: Actually, the book I want to talk about
you do not rise to the level of your goals
So yes, this is the book that

B2.title: Describe a book that has influenced you
B2.body: There is a book I find myself recommending to people almost every month, and that is Atomic Habits by James Clear. I first read it roughly two years ago, during a period when I felt I was working hard but going nowhere in particular. A friend, who could see this more clearly than I could, more or less handed it to me and told me to take it seriously. On the surface, it is a practical book about how to build good habits and break bad ones, but I think what makes it so popular is something deeper. Clear's central claim is that progress is far less about willpower or motivation than people imagine. It is about designing your environment and your identity so that the desired behaviour becomes the easiest, most obvious thing to do. He frames this with the famous one percent better every day idea, which sounds almost trivial until you do the maths and realise how dramatically small daily gains compound over a year. What I took away most strongly was the emphasis on identity. Instead of focusing on what I wanted to achieve, he encouraged readers to ask what kind of person they want to become, and then prove it with small, daily actions. That shift sounds subtle, but for me it has been quietly transformative. I went from telling myself I should exercise more, to thinking of myself as someone who simply does not skip a workout on weekdays. I write a little every morning, not because I am chasing a particular book, but because, well, that is who I am now. So this is the book I would describe as the one that has most influenced how I think about long-term change.

key_phrases.phrase: more or less handed it to me
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 사실상 그것을 내 손에 쥐어 주었다

key_phrases.phrase: What I took away most strongly was
well, that is who I am now

C1.title: Describe a book that has influenced you
C1.body: The book I would like to talk about is Atomic Habits by James Clear, and I would describe it as the one that has most quietly reshaped the way I approach long-term change. I first picked it up about two years ago, in a stretch of life where I felt perpetually busy without actually moving forward on anything I truly cared about. A close friend, who had clearly noticed this before I had, pressed it into my hands and told me, in no uncertain terms, to give it a proper read. On the surface, Atomic Habits is shelved as productivity, and Clear does provide an unusually clean framework for designing habits, with his four laws of making behaviour obvious, attractive, easy and satisfying. But I think the reason it has resonated so widely, and certainly with me, is that it reframes change as an architectural problem rather than a motivational one. The argument is not that you need more willpower, but that you need to redesign your environment and your sense of self so that the desired behaviour becomes the path of least resistance. The line that has stayed with me most is that you do not rise to the level of your goals, you fall to the level of your systems. What truly influenced me, though, was the emphasis on identity-based habits. Clear argues that every action you take is essentially a vote for the kind of person you wish to become, and that durable change happens when the behaviour and the identity start reinforcing each other. That subtle reframing has had an outsized effect on me. Rather than declaring grand intentions, I started running tiny, identity-anchored experiments. I write for fifteen minutes every morning, not to finish any particular project, but because I have decided I am a person who writes daily. I exercise in modest, almost embarrassingly small sessions, but I do so consistently. Over two years, those compounded micro-decisions have produced changes I would have thought required some dramatic burst of motivation. So if I had to name a book that genuinely altered the texture of my daily life, this would be it. It did not give me a new goal. It gave me a new way of becoming.

key_phrases.meaning_ko: 내 손에 그것을 꾹 쥐어 주었다
내게 지나칠 정도로 큰 영향을 미친
